---

Hi guys and welcome to the Goddess Project. Today we're going to be having a practice session around being one's own best friend. So what this really comes down to is how do you speak to yourself? And when you're working with a client, how do you help her or help him get into that space of understanding that they can talk to themselves in a better way, that they can actually love themselves and they can be a source of encouragement, a source of connection for themselves. So I think if I give nothing else to my clients, this is the most important life skill I can possibly help a client with. And for some of us it sounds so simple and for other of us it's actually one of the hardest things we could ever do. And one of the biggest changes we could make on the inside is to speak to ourselves in a loving way, to actually be our own closest dearest friend, to know ourselves on the inside and to love that person. So I would invite you to use this idea at any time in your coaching work with a client. And I would introduce it to a client by saying, I'd like to learn more about how you speak to yourself. You know, most of us actually speak to ourselves in a meaner voice and say meaner things than we would ever say to our worst enemy. And when we begin to uncover what it is we're actually saying to ourselves, it can be shocking. So of course we don't want to bring our client down and we don't want to introduce this to somebody who hears voices or is very imbalanced. But I think it's it's a fantastic strategy to use when someone is very hard on themselves. They're very driven and judgmental. I like to ask them about that inner voice. And I'd like I like to invite them to want to make that change. So I would say, let's say they just told me about how critical they are. I might I might elevate them first and say, you know, I think the way you talk to yourself is actually just the way someone who's highly intelligent talks to herself. Because being critical is a sign of intelligence. However, oh, and that was Liam that just went by because I am a mom and I am a mom working at home. So so I would elevate by saying that criticism is actually intelligence. But then I would ask, would you like to do a practice where you learn to speak to yourself in a nicer way? Now let's start off with the moments where this could serve you the most. And so I'd like to tell you a story. And I'd like you as a coach to come up with your own stories. Feel free to use mine, but also come up with your own for this. Now my story is a true story about when I realized that this inner voice could serve me. I had recently been studying some Buddhism, listening to some talks about compassion, self and compassion. And I almost crashed my car. I was driving down the street. And the car in front of me hit their brakes. I hit my brakes. I slid my heart accelerated. I panicked. And I started to say really mean things to myself. Oh my God, you almost killed everyone behind you. How could you do that? You know, I can't believe you would make that mistake. And then I remember it. It kicked in and I remembered this practice of loving myself and showing up as my own friend. And I thought to myself, would I ever say this to a girlfriend that had almost crashed her car? No, I would never. And in that moment, I suddenly went into another place and I said to myself, it's going to be okay. And I just kept repeating to myself as my body relaxed, my heart was going so fast, I was full of adrenaline. I kept telling myself, it's going to be okay. And just that it's a really powerful statement to use on yourself. It's going to be okay. And you know, oftentimes we're asked, how do I work with a client who's overwhelmed or has anxiety or panic attacks? Learning this inner voice, learning to be your own best friend and give yourself comfort. It's one of the biggest strategies you can use to help overcome anxiety. So to plan with your client today, when and how he or she can talk to themselves in a loving way, what they could say. And you might want to get very specific. They might tell you, you know, well, when I procrastinate, I start saying this to myself, I start saying, you know what, you're just never going to do it. You're never going to do it. Look at those words. And let's reshape them. Let's create a new statement. And let's practice you saying your old statement. And now re-saying it in this new way. So instead of saying, I'm never going to do it, I'm never going to do it. Why don't you say, I'd really like to do this. Sometimes I think I'll never do it. And sometimes I know that I will do it. But I sure would like to do it. Doesn't that feel good? So that's the kind of way we can reframe and pretend. And through reframing and pretending, we develop this core, really key skill of being our own best friend. So work together on this, have a great time practicing this. And please practice this on yourself. This is the most important strategy that I can give you as a tool for overcoming your own fears, your own anxieties, your own limiting beliefs, and limiting thoughts, and patterns, and anxieties. Become your own best friend. Talk to yourself in a kind way. This will kick in in times of great stress. This will kick in when you're having an argument with someone else. You'll remember to be loving to yourself. And then that will help you be loving to them. So please, please work on this today and beyond today with yourself.
